children.text: The Panama Canal depends on a source it cannot control: freshwater. These were the words used by Ricaurte Vásquez, head of the Panama Canal Authority (ACP), to describe the systemic vulnerability of the waterway. The Canal operates within a hydrological regime shaped by climate-induced droughts, increasing global shipping demand, and growing municipal water consumption. In navigating these pressures, its infrastructural limits are now extending into rural watersheds, particularly those of the Caribbean basin, including Río Indio, one of the most ecologically and socially significant rivers under current evaluation for the development of a new water reservoir.

This AVAnnotate project examines the competing epistemologies through which the Canal is governed and contested, focusing on the legacies of its expansion processes and the ACP proposed dam and reservoir project known as "Water for the Future".
This project approaches the Panama Canal as an archive in motion, where infrastructure, memory, and displacement continuously produce one another.
By assembling media, policy discourse, and narrative interventions,
traces the mechanisms through which water becomes a medium of governance, and how testimony becomes a site where competing empistemologies of territory ar articulated.
